Just saved that screenshot of the pinned group chat to house/docs, naming it “guest_rules_2022-11-11.” My hand shook a little as I typed out the effective date, but I still added a small note in my memos: “I can sleep more soundly now.” It sounds a bit silly, but in that moment, it really was just a massive sense of relief.

My roommate brought someone over again this morning; what started as polite small talk has turned into a habit of frequent visits. The sound of them laughing and fooling around in the hallway woke up my microphone several times during my early morning recording session (I had planned to set up my room to a minimum recording standard over the weekend to get some extra material done). After the guest left, he even said sarcastically, “Is it really a problem if my friend comes over just once?” I really wanted to snap back right then, or just delete those interrupted clips and start over. But I told myself to do what I learned in therapy—isolate the emotions into actionable items first.

I kept my voice low and just repeated the facts: the timing, the source of the noise, and the activities that were affected. Then I suggested a clear timeframe (no guests after 8 PM on Fridays) and an adjustment to the cleaning rotation. I tried hard to make every sentence sound like a rule rather than a reproach. My roommate’s prickly tone made me a bit anxious again, but once I wrote it down as a rule—clearly defining responsibilities and time slots—my anxiety actually lessened. Maybe it’s a bad habit of mine—soothing myself through documentation—but for now, it seems to be working.

I still double-checked everything three times before sending the rules to the group and pinning the screenshot (the old habit, a neurotic flare-up). After archiving it, I wrote down how I felt in my notes: *I can sleep soundly now.* It’s strange; even when my energy is at a 3/10, these little ritualistic actions still give me a sense of security. It also proves that my recent "hybrid strategy"—relying on a few short-term paid projects for cash flow while carving out a small window each day for creative work—is viable. I just sent the delivery screenshot for Monday’s milestone payment to the client, with the note “Delivered.” A bit more peace of mind.

Next steps:
- Back up the pinned group chat screenshot to the cloud (it’s in house/docs, but add a copy to Dropbox).
- Update the cleaning rotation calendar (mark Friday nights as "Quiet Slots").
- Edit the interrupted recordings from today into quick samples, labeled “Recorded/Needs Post-production.”
- Set aside a two-hour focus window for myself tomorrow; don't let anyone drag me out to socialize.

I know I’ll keep checking these records over and over, and I’ll probably glance at my notes one last time before bed, but at least for now, things have been converted into actionable items. They’re no longer just a blur of emotions churning in my chest. Keep at it. Don't let the small things break you.

September 11, 2023, 11:00 AM

Just got off the phone with Dad. We’ve scheduled his physical for Saturday—he agreed to a full check-up at our usual hospital. I opened my budget spreadsheet and read it out to him line by line: rent, transfers to my parents, various subscriptions... and then the estimated cost for the physical. I felt a wave of shame, but I still made the verbal commitment over the phone to transfer 2,000 yuan first as a deposit. My voice trembled slightly as I said it, as if I were confirming to myself that I’m still holding it together.

I’m feeling so conflicted. Being able to make things concrete and read the numbers aloud gives me a tiny bit of relief—at least it’s no longer just a vague fear. But then a different kind of tension kicks in: this money means I’ll have to watch my cash flow even more obsessively over the next two weeks. I know I get insomnia whenever I’m stressed; last night, I checked my bank records three times, and before bed, I kept spiraling through "what ifs." The scariest thought is: if I can’t pull through, the guilt will be unbearable. Saying it out loud feels cold, like a stone pressing down on my chest.

I understand where these emotions are coming from. The income fluctuations over the past few months, the abnormalities in my parents' previous health checks, and a work rhythm that feels constantly interrupted—it’s all kept me in a state of high alert. But today, I did one small thing: I listed the physical as a priority payment for this month. Not just as a vague intention, but as an actual priority in the budget sheet. I’ve marked it in `budget_recovery_2023.xlsx`: *Priority 1—Parents' Physical (2,000 deposit)*, followed by rent, then the essential subscriptions. The moment I wrote it down, my anxiety finally had a place to land.

At the same time, there’s a sense of shame: why do I always wait until I’m at my breaking point to start making spreadsheets and setting priorities? Why do I wait for anxiety to turn my life upside down before doing these "obvious" things? This self-reproach is like an old sweater—uncomfortable to wear, but hard to take off. I have to remind myself that the sleep intervention and weekly counseling I started recently aren't for show; I need to stick with them. The "no emails before bed" rule must be upheld, even if it’s just one small step every three days.

My commitment to myself is specific: transfer that 2,000 this afternoon (no more "maybe tomorrow"), set a calendar reminder for Saturday’s appointment, and confirm with my parents on Friday if they need me to go with them. I also need to rewrite my buffer goal for next week’s living expenses, down to the exact figures and timing. Doing this isn't about being brave; it’s about being pragmatic. I need these external little rules to fight off the panic that comes at night.

 I feel a bit tired now—low on energy, but clear-headed. Writing this down feels like I’ve offloaded some of the anxiety onto the paper. I hope Saturday’s check-up gives me some feedback that puts my mind at ease, and I hope I don’t trip up again on this seemingly simple path.

To be honest, the lesson remains the same: relying on others for temporary fixes only amplifies my insecurity. Rather than asking my father for a short-term loan (I still remember the complicated feelings from last time), what I really want is an automated buffer: a fixed emergency fund, clear billing boundaries, and delivery templates that reduce redundant communication. I’m writing this here to remind myself: when anxiety strikes, break it down into small steps, then get to work. Writing down tomorrow’s most important task on a sticky note before bed tonight seems to help me let go just a little bit.

Ming, I just posted that long piece and got some really heartwarming comments, so I wanted to reach out and share a few thoughts privately. It’s 2:00 PM on October 10th, and I just left that cafe nearby. I’d originally set aside two hours today for my personal experiments, but several times throughout, I felt this overwhelming urge to reply to client emails immediately. My hand actually slid toward my phone more than once. Eventually, I switched to recording voice memos or jotting down bullet points of what I wanted to say first, then forcing myself back into the creative work. It was slightly more effective than just diving into the emails, but I could still feel that "tug-of-war" tension.

This whole experience makes me feel even more strongly that I need to "lock down" my creative blocks—treating them as non-negotiable resources rather than just "available gaps" that can be filled at will. But I’m conflicted: I worry that if I seal off my time too strictly, I’ll lose deals or hurt my income (you know I’m still relying on a few retained clients and revenue-sharing for my safety net). Yet, if I don’t protect those half-day experiments, I’m afraid I’ll slowly grind away the things that create long-term value.

I wanted to ask you two things—be blunt with me:
1) Do you think locking down fixed blocks (like Tuesday mornings or Friday afternoons) is realistic? Are there any areas where you think I’m likely to compromise that I should define clearly in advance (e.g., what actually counts as an "emergency" exception)?
2) Could you be my "accountability buddy" for a bit? For example, if you see me replying to emails late at night, just drop a "Locked!" message. Or, I could message you before I start a locked session, and you could help me hold the line on not replying to non-urgent messages for 24 hours before bed? I won't bug you all the time; even just once or twice would be incredibly helpful. I’d love to buy you a coffee or send a small "red envelope" as a thank you.

Just for context: I’ve already automated some of my income splits and kept a few long-term clients as my baseline. My psychological check-ins also suggested I practice setting boundaries, so I want to try a real "locked-down" experimental period to see if I can get my creative efficiency and mental state back on track. I just feel more at ease saying this out loud, so I’m coming to you to vent a little and ask for advice.

Be straight with me—if you’d rather not be the "supervisor," just tell me so I can drop the idea. Thanks for always being there. — Zi'ang

September 14, 2023, Afternoon — Just a quick note

The Event:
I just sent the contract to the client and requested a 30% deposit; they replied and agreed. In that moment, I leaned back in my chair and felt a genuine flash of relief—like a small stone had been lifted off my chest, if only for a moment. A few minutes later, I couldn't help but pull out my phone to check my bank account, terrified the transfer might be delayed or that I’d miss the notification. My palms started getting clammy again.

The Feeling:
That brief sense of ease was quickly replaced by my habitual hyper-vigilance. I want to celebrate, yet I’m afraid of celebrating too early. One part of me is telling myself this is a step forward in my professional process, while the other is obsessively double-checking numbers—it feels like I’m arguing with myself.

Next Steps:
1) Set a follow-up reminder for the payment now (48/72 hours) and put it at the very top of Sunday night’s to-do list.
2) Send the client one more message regarding the receipt process once the payment is confirmed. Keep the language direct and templated (don't let myself get caught in conversational loops).
3) No more checking the bank app before bed. If I feel the urge, switch to my sleep app routine and bedtime notes. Write down the single most important task for tomorrow, then turn off the phone.

Self-Reflection:
There is progress—I managed to ask for a deposit and got a "yes." But the old habits are still there; I need to rely on rules to externalize and manage these emotions. Tonight, I’ll just follow the process as planned. Don't let myself get dragged back into mindless screen-refreshing.

Subject: Updates to Payment and Contracting Procedures for Products and Custom Projects (Effective Date Below)

Hello,

I am preparing to launch my Urban Ambient Sound Pack and Mobile Icon Pack for sale soon. To prevent future misunderstandings and to protect both delivery and cash flow, I am adjusting my payment and contracting procedures as follows:

1) Off-the-shelf Digital Products (e.g., sound packs, icon packs): As "ready-to-buy" items, these will follow an instant payment/digital delivery process with no deposit required. Download links and licensing instructions will be issued automatically upon purchase.
2) Custom Services (UI/Sound design, specialized consulting, etc.): These will require a 30% deposit followed by milestone-based payments. Delivery milestones and acceptance criteria will be clearly defined in the contract. I have attached a contract template (contract_deposit_template_20240914.docx) for your reference.
3) Financial Note: To enhance operational stability, I plan to allocate 20% of initial sales revenue into an emergency fund to cover short-term cash fluctuations and guarantee delivery. If you have specific requirements regarding these terms, we can specify them within the contract.

To minimize the impact on existing collaborations, this policy will officially take effect in two weeks (September 28, 2024). During this period, or for long-term clients, I can honor previous agreements or negotiate a separate transition plan. If you wish to continue our cooperation or need to adjust terms for current/upcoming projects, please reply to this email or contact me via WeChat/phone within the next two weeks. This will allow me to prepare a customized contract version and confirm the timeline for you.

Thank you for your understanding and support. I look forward to our continued collaboration.

Best regards,

Ziang Lin

Subject: Project Contract (Milestones & Payment Terms) — 50% Initial Payment Due Within 7 Days

Hello,

Following our conversation and the revisions made with the assistance of legal counsel, I have incorporated the milestones and late payment penalties into the contract (see attached). I have summarized the key terms below for your confirmation.

**Key Terms:**
*   **Milestone 1 (Requirement Confirmation & Proposal/Kick-off):** 50% upfront payment. Please remit payment and send back the transfer receipt within 7 days of contract signing (work will commence upon receipt of funds).
*   **Milestone 2 (Mid-term Delivery):** 30%, payable within 7 days of delivery.
*   **Milestone 3 (Final Delivery & Acceptance):** 20%, payable within 7 days of acceptance.

**Late Payments:** A late fee of 0.05% per day will be charged on any outstanding balance. If payment is overdue by more than 14 days, we reserve the right to suspend delivery and initiate arbitration or terminate the contract as per the agreement.

If you prefer an installment plan, please submit a written schedule specifying the dates and conditions for each payment within 7 days. Please note that proof of the initial payment remains a prerequisite for starting the work.

Please confirm and send back the payment receipt or provide written feedback within 7 days. I will arrange the next steps once the receipt is received.

Best regards,

Ziang Lin

I just received a screenshot and a public comment from a buyer, and it felt like being tossed into a small whirlpool. My chest tightened for no reason, and my mind kept replaying the words of that comment. For the next two hours, I couldn't focus on anything else—constantly refreshing the backend download counts, checking notifications, and re-checking file names, as if searching for some piece of evidence that would put my mind at ease. My old habit kicked in again: whenever I’m anxious, I have this compulsive urge to fix things immediately.

I took a few steps to turn that emotion into action. First, I replied to the buyer, thanking them politely and requesting more specific screenshots of the error. Then, I verified the download logs for that version in the backend and identified potential volume or editing break points. I uploaded the corrected sample pack labeled as v1.1, extended the preview clip on the product page by a few seconds, and made the description regarding the preview requirements clearer. Finally, I logged the income in my bookkeeping app and tagged it according to my set ratios (hoping to prevent money from becoming an additional source of anxiety).

My reflection: I need a packaging checklist so I don't have to rely on these last-minute sprints to patch holes every time. I’ve jotted down a few initial points: file naming conventions, standards for preview length and volume, concise product copy, quick-access download screenshots and version numbers, and monitoring alerts for the first hour after uploading. I know I’m not a fan of tedious processes, but being called out publicly these few times has made me want to turn the workflow into a repeatable ritual—just like how I write down the most important task for the next day on a sticky note before bed.

My chest is still echoing with tension, but I feel a bit more grounded after taking action. I need to turn this checklist into a template tomorrow and stop using anxiety as my primary fuel for fixing things... I'm going to brew a cup of coffee now and give myself ten minutes away from the backend.

*   **Includes:** Premium check-up package (specialized tumor markers/imaging, etc.) + higher insurance coverage + a larger emergency fund.

  - Goal: Define technical indicators for the comparison sheet to prove I've done my homework to friends/collaborators.
  - Key Takeaways (Notes): Priorities: 24-bit/96k (96k is nice to have but not mandatory), low-noise preamps (ideally S/N > 100 dB), external mic support (XLR & 48V), easy file export (USB-C or Mass Storage mode), power type (AA vs. internal lithium), size/weight is crucial for hiking/sampling.

23:45 — Opened Audio Forums/Zhihu/Niche Reviews (Keywords: portable recorder field sampling practical use)
  - Collected Insights: H1n/DR-05X are great for entry-level/lightweight sampling; H4n/H5 are more reliable for field interviews and multi-track recording; Sony’s internal mics are "crisper" but less flexible; if planning to make long-term SFX packs, prioritize models with XLR or those compatible with high-quality condenser mics.

23:52 — Search: sampling mic suggestions external small condenser mobile recording comparison
  - Consideration: Should I buy a portable recorder + cheap small-diaphragm condenser (e.g., RODE Lavalier / compact condenser) or go straight for an H4n/H5 with a high-quality handheld condenser?
  - Budget Estimate Notes: Target budget set at ¥800 - ¥2,000; if going for XLR + condenser mic, total cost will hit ¥2,500 - ¥5,000 (the upper limit of what's acceptable short-term).

00:00 — Search: SD card specs recording 24-bit 96k recommended speed class
  - Conclusion Notes: Buy at least 64GB Class 10 U3, preferably a 128GB backup card; files are large, and swapping cards in the field is faster than transferring data.

00:05 — Search: windscreen deadcat portable mini field recording price
  - Notes: Wind noise is the biggest enemy of outdoor sampling; getting a proper windscreen is a higher priority than a "more expensive" device upgrade.

00:12 — Opened Bilibili/YouTube Reviews: Zoom H4n Pro vs Tascam DR-40X vs Sony PCM-A10 (Watch/Notes)
  - Listening Notes: H4n has slightly thicker lows; Sony has more detail in the highs; DR-40X soundstage is quite neutral.
  - Decision Lean: Buy a high-value model with external mic support (H4n or Tascam are safer bets); if budget is tight, start with H4n (or DR-40X) + add external mic/windscreen later.

00:22 — Search: used Zoom H5 H4n Taobao/Xianyu prices
  - Goal: Lower the budget if a used unit is in good condition with verifiable warranty.
  - Notes: Used H5 approx. ¥1,200 - ¥1,600; H4n Pro approx. ¥700 - ¥1,000 (depending on condition).

00:28 — Personal To-Do List (Short Notes):
  - Priority 1 (Must): Finalize target model (portable + XLR support) → H4n Pro / DR-40X / H5 (pick one).
  - Priority 2 (Accessories): 64/128GB SD card, mini deadcat, spare AA batteries/power bank (depending on power source).
  - Priority 3 (Optional): Small-diaphragm condenser or handheld dynamic (decide on used vs. new based on budget).

00:33 — Search: comparison of buying channels JD/Tmall/Xianyu warranty price final cost
  - Conclusion Notes: For new units, prioritize JD/Tmall (invoice + warranty); Xianyu for price negotiation but requires inspection and return conditions; keep price screenshots for friends/family to show research was done (to explain if needed).

00:38 — Final Search: "portable recorder final decision table template" / "recording project sampling schedule template"
  - Goal: Turn the selection, budget, and milestones into a shareable simple table (less explaining when showing collaborators).
  - Short Plan (For myself):
    - Next Monday: Lock model / Place order (or contact used seller).
    - Next Wednesday: All accessories ready (card, windscreen, cables).
    - Next Weekend: First field recording (2-hour scene test: park / street market / cafe).

October 27, 2025  20:00

Tonight feels a bit like launching a small boat into the water—nerve-wracking, yet a bit of a relief. I listed the sound effects pack this evening and sent out the first batch of marketing emails. My palms were still trembling slightly the moment I hit send. Out of habit, I went straight to the data: I’ve saved screenshots of the email open rates and purchase links, thinking I can look back at them later as proof that this wasn't just a pipe dream. The act of saving these screenshots calmed me down briefly; it felt like creating a backup for my anxiety.

I also reached out to friends to ask for help with word-of-mouth sharing. I told them quite bluntly: "Productization at least gives me a bit of a passive buffer." Saying it out loud felt like self-consolation, but it’s also the truth—if everything depends on freelance gigs, I’m constantly at the mercy of time and client whims. Putting something up on a platform that can be sold repeatedly, even if the income is modest, creates a buffer zone. It gives me more leverage to set boundaries when negotiating contracts. Hearing myself say this, I felt a mix of pragmatic calculation and naive hope, like I’m betting on something small and steady to slowly ferment.

The concept of "boundaries" has been looping in my head these past few weeks. Insisting on deposits, clarifying milestones, the discomfort of saying "no"—these things might cost me a few opportunities in the short term, but I can feel the security that comes with that sense of control, even if it’s just psychological. I know I’m prone to staying up late and double-checking everything when faced with uncertainty, so I’m trying to let external rules make the decisions for me. That way, my emotions don't get dragged away every single time.

I’m still torn: Should I invest more time into productization? Should I push another round of emails? I know promotion requires patience and a consistent rhythm, and I have a tendency to lose steam quickly. My plan for tomorrow morning is to check the performance of this email batch first thing, draft a reply template for those who might reach out privately, and send a few DMs to trusted friends as a reminder to share (not a bombardment, just a gentle ask for help).

I’m writing this by the bedside lamp. There are still some stray drafts and takeout boxes on the desk—a bit messy, as usual, but that’s just my reality. Before bed, I’ll write the most important task for tomorrow on a sticky note: turn the promotion data into a one-page summary. It won't be perfect, but at least I got the work out there today. That’s worth acknowledging.

3) Grab a physical book, sit at the head of the bed or in a corner with warm lighting, and read for at least 30 minutes (the goal is relaxation, not information consumption).
4) Write down the "Most Important Thing for Tomorrow" on a bedside sticky note (short sentence), and list any "Priority Payment/Collection Items" on the second line for immediate handling in the morning.
5) Before sleep, take a screenshot of the nightly routine record from the sleep app and save it to: health/sleep_logs/2023-10 (as evidence for review).
6) If the urge to check the phone arises during a brief nighttime awakening, remind yourself: The internet is off, the phone is in the drawer; take 3 deep breaths before returning to bed.

- [ ] Wi-Fi/Data turned off
- [ ] Phone placed in drawer / face down
- [ ] Physical book out and read
- [ ] Tomorrow's Most Important Thing written down (including payment priorities)
- [ ] Sleep app screenshot saved to designated folder

Notes:
- This is a private ritual; do not share. Relying on external triggers is more stable than willpower; try not to break the rules due to temporary anxiety. In case of emergencies (urgent client matters), note it on the sticky note and try to leave a written/email trail before handling.

2024-10-10 11:00 — Memo (Podcast Discount Request)

Background: The podcast client has requested a one-time discount. I am documenting the counter-arguments and compromise options here to serve as a future pricing policy template.

1) Key Arguments Against a One-Time Discount
- Limits future pricing leverage: A single concession is easily treated as a benchmark, making it difficult to return to original rates in subsequent negotiations.
- Sets a precedent: They or others may cite the "previous discount" as a precedent during future collaborations or renewals.
- Impacts brand perception: Constant price-cutting lowers the perceived value of my work (shifting from a "premium series" to "cheap sound effects/assets").
- Affects financial planning: Discounts reduce my ability to reinvest initial earnings into the Creative Fund (established principle: 20% of initial revenue is reinvested).
- Operational risk: Without a contract, milestones, or a deposit, discounts are more likely to result in bad debt or delayed payments.

2) Compromise Options (Client-Facing)
- Discount Cap: Maximum 10% (framed as "up to 10%" for negotiation leverage).
- Scope: Valid for "First Collaboration/First Episode Purchase" only; does not automatically apply to subsequent episodes.
- Validity Period: Quote is valid for 30 days after signing (reverts to original price upon expiration).
- Prerequisites: Requires a signed contract and a deposit (30% recommended); installment payments based on milestones.
- Exposure/Reciprocity: Require credit in the show notes/intro + a link to the program on the product/demo page (as exchangeable value).
- Explicit Terms: The contract must state that "this discount is a one-time offer and shall not serve as a basis for future pricing," and cannot be cited as a floor price for renewals.

3) Stakeholders / Signatories
- Signatories: Myself (Lin Zi'ang) + Head of Podcast (for confirmation).
- Payee: Myself (Bank details/payment path specified in the contract).
- CC/Review: Online Legal Counsel (for contract template review) + Accountant/Bookkeeper (for tracking revenue and reinvestment).
- Internal Archiving: Save the final contract and correspondence in the "Pricing" folder. Filename: pricing_podcast_v1 (use _v2/_final for subsequent versions).

4) Next Steps (Shortlist)
- Draft the above terms into a one-page "Podcast Collaboration—First-Time Discount" template (naming as above).
- Reply to the podcast client using a combination of "Limited-time offer + Deposit + Credit in exchange for discount" (confirm verbally first, then send the contract).
- Submit the contract template for legal review (within 48 hours); do not commence work until the deposit is received.
- Upon receiving the first payment, transfer 20% to the Creative Reinvestment Account and archive the screenshot (to prove policy feasibility).

Note (Self-Reflection): My preference remains not to discount. However, by applying controlled conditions, I can frame this as a one-time marketing effort rather than a long-term habit. I need to finalize this template now to prevent temporary compromises from becoming the new norm.

**One-Liner Observation:** The client wanted flexible payment → I held the line using data and templates.

**Meeting Highlights**
- **Client Focus:** Cash flow flexibility, clear acceptance/refund terms, and a fast kickoff.
- **My Approach:** I started with explanatory statements, but after seeing limited results, I pivoted: I presented contract clauses, past invoices/payment screenshots, milestone schedules, and first-buyer review data.
- **Effective Evidence:** Historical payment screenshots, clear milestone templates (Delivery → Acceptance → Payment), simple risk/cost comparison tables, and user testimonial screenshots.
- **Key Concerns:** Whether they can pay-as-they-go without delaying the kickoff; how defaults/delays are handled.

**Self-Assessment (Brief)**
- **Strategic Progress:** Shifted from "storytelling" to "letting the evidence speak"—a more mature negotiation style.
- **Emotions:** Still felt some anxiety (racing heart, fear of being lowballed), but the tension eased once the evidence was on the table.
- **Energy:** Moderate. Structured note-taking significantly helps stabilize my mood.

**Next Steps (Reusable Productized Workflow)**
1. **Create a "Negotiation Kit" (Reusable):** Contract templates (30% deposit / milestone payment terms), acceptance checklists, and an FAQ regarding flexible payment terms and examples.
2. **Add a Payment Option Comparison Table to the product page/quote card:** Milestone-based (Standard), Lump-sum (includes 10% risk premium), and On-demand (requires additional security deposit/extended delivery window).
3. **Draft a short negotiation script/price card** for quick replication in the future.
4. **Organize invoices, payment screenshots, and first-buyer reviews into an Evidence Library** for instant access.
5. **Automated Reminders:** Set up invoice templates + milestone due reminders (using existing tools for now).

**Bottom Line & Risk Control**
- No zero-deposit projects or long-term credit without milestones.
- If the client insists on single payments below the minimum threshold, demand a higher total price or reject the order.
- Maintain the "right to walk away"—don't sacrifice cash flow just to keep a client.

**Execution Schedule (Short-term)**
- **Today:** Archive the evidence screenshots used in the meeting (30 mins).
- **Tomorrow Morning (Sticky note reminder):** Refine the contract templates (30 mins).
- **This Week:** Compile the Negotiation Kit into a reusable one-pager and upload it to my resources folder.

**Emotional Memo (To Self)**
- Every time I standardize a process, I eliminate a source of future anxiety.
- This experience makes me feel like I’m "growing up" professionally, but I still need to practice not letting emotions drive the negotiation.
- Go grab a coffee. Before bed, expand this into a case note, but don't spend too long on it.

March 30, 2024 – Evening

After work today, I finally put an end to something I’d been hesitating over: I set up a monthly auto-transfer for my emergency fund online. The amount isn't huge, but it’s fixed now. I took a screenshot of the confirmation page, saved it as "Finance/Emergency_Fund/Auto_Transfer_20240330.png," and sent the screenshot along with a brief explanation to my dad as a quick update. Strangely, I felt a sense of relief after telling him. It’s as if I’ve handed over a recurring, magnifying worry to a machine to handle.

This actually ties back to my recent thoughts on payment discipline: deposit contracts are useful, but I can’t rely on hope alone; I have to institutionalize the things I can control. Setting up this auto-transfer is also an exercise in turning anxiety into traceable evidence—screenshots, email records, line items in a budget sheet. My energy levels are stable for now, but the pressure hasn't fully dissipated; I still worry about system errors or forgetting to adjust the amount.

Next steps: Check on Monday if the first payment actually went through (grab a screenshot of the transaction record). If successful, update the entry in the monthly expenses tab of "Budget_v2.xlsx" and add a quarterly check-in to my calendar reminders. Also, my therapist suggested I keep using these small rituals to narrow down uncertainty—maybe try listing the next things to automate so my sense of security doesn't hinge on a few manual tasks.

Writing this down makes me feel more grounded. Before bed, I’ve written the most important task for tomorrow on a sticky note: check the first transfer. It’s a good feeling—at least tonight I don’t have to keep refreshing the bank page.

Hey Ming, sorry to bother you—I just got off a video call with Wu Zhiyuan and wanted to give you a quick update, and honestly, ask for some advice/reassurance.

The main points of the call were pretty straightforward: for the first time, I took the initiative to bring up putting deposits and milestones into the contract. I explained that milestone-based payments and an upfront deposit would help mitigate cash flow risks. He didn’t reject it outright; he seemed understanding and agreed to try it out with a specific ratio—30% upfront, with the rest tied to milestones. I’ve saved the draft as `contracts/2023-06_wu_deposit.pdf` and am waiting for you to take a look before I send it over.

The problem is—I kept using this apologetic tone throughout the call, constantly saying things like "Sorry, things are a bit tight lately." The more I said it, the more ashamed I felt. I keep worrying that I might lose a long-term client because of this. To be honest, my energy and mental state are both pretty shot right now (you know I haven't been sleeping well), and I’m struggling with the tension between trying to professionalize my workflow and the fear of being seen as unreliable. It feels really embarrassing.

I wanted to ask you two very specific things:
1) Do you think a 30% deposit is reasonable? In your experience, with a long-term freelance client like Wu, can I push for 50%? Or is 30% a safer bet for now?

   — Attaching the draft (`contracts/2023-06_wu_deposit.pdf`) for his confirmation or feedback.
   — Explaining the plan to trial the 30% deposit and including payment info in the contract.
   — Keeping the tone professional but not cold.
I didn't paste the draft here to avoid a wall of text, but if you have a moment, could you check the contract and the email draft (or I can just send you the PDF/draft directly)? Let me know if any phrasing sounds too apologetic/weak, or too aggressive, and how you’d fix it.

Also, I’m kind of hitting a wall emotionally. I feel like by constantly apologizing, I’m basically admitting I’m unreliable. Thanks, reply whenever.

- **Tools:** Ableton Live + iZotope RX + FabFilter Pro-Q + Valhalla
- **Output:** 30s demo loop (wav + mp3), 2 percussion stems.
- **Cost/Risk Note:** If cleaner impacts are needed, may require re-recording with a shotgun mic or field recorder. Estimated equipment advance ~800–1,500 RMB or hiring help at 300–600 RMB/session.

**2) Cafe Corner — "Low-end Vocal Ambience / Chewing & Cup Vibrations"**
- **Description:** Close-up low-frequency whispers, cup clinks, background grinding; overall warm and thick.
- **Post Ideas:** Extract low-end vocals for an ambient pad (low-pass + blurred granulation); use cup sounds as high-frequency rhythmic accents; create a "vocal atmosphere pad" for scoring.
- **Workflow Example:** High-pass to remove wind noise → Dual-track duplication (one for granular blurred pad, one for natural processing) → Low-end saturation (Saturator) → Light sidechain → Export multiple versions (dry / wet / pad only).
- **Priority:** 1 (Strong "portfolio" feel)
- **Est. Time:** Org 1h / Granular design 2h / Mixing 1h = 4h
- **Tools:** Ableton Live (Granulator) + Valhalla + FabFilter
- **Output:** 45s ambient demo, pad stem, cup-clink loop.
- **Cost/Risk Note:** Mixing est. 3–4h. Willing to absorb labor costs short-term, but outsourcing mix would be ~300–600 RMB/track.

**3) Old Neighborhood Stairwell — "Creaky Wooden Doors / Footstep Echoes"**
- **Description:** Long-tail reverb, wood friction, distant children's laughter (can be used for ethereal layers).
- **Post Ideas:** Capture the long tail for spatial showcase; extend echoes and create layered loops (foreground footsteps, mid-ground wood friction, background pitched-down laughter).
- **Workflow Example:** Trim → Time-stretch tail → Replace with characterful "dirty room" IR convolution reverb → Layered arrangement.
- **Priority:** 2 (Usable material for demo pack)
- **Est. Time:** Org 0.8h / Processing 1.5h / Mixing 0.5h = 2.8h
- **Tools:** Reaper/Ableton + IR convolution + iZotope
- **Output:** 3 loops (short/med/long) for scoring or soundscapes.
- **Risk:** Stretching may introduce artifacts requiring RX repair (extra 0.5–1h).

**4) Market Stall — "Plastic Bag Rustle + Street Vendor Vocal Snippets"**
- **Description:** High-frequency rustling, localized vocal fragments (low intelligibility but rhythmic).
- **Post Ideas:** Keep vocal fragments for rhythmic slicing (slice to MIDI); use plastic rustle as high-end ear candy; combine into a percussive loop.
- **Workflow Example:** Trim → Chop to slices (Ableton) → Rearrange into 16/32-step patterns → Add delay/filters.
- **Priority:** 2
- **Est. Time:** Org 1h / Slicing & Arrangement 2h / Mixing 1h = 4h
- **Tools:** Ableton Live, FabFilter, Valhalla
- **Output:** Rhythm pack (4 loops + 2 one-shots).

**5) Back-alley Pipes/Drainage — "Low-frequency Pulse / Heartbeat Loop"**
- **Description:** Rhythmic low-end pulses with potential as ambient bass or kick alternatives.
- **Post Ideas:** Low-cut processing followed by sidechain pulsing, or use as a base waveform for a synth to create sub-loops.
- **Priority:** 3
- **Est. Time:** Org 0.5h / Processing 1h / Mixing 0.5h = 2h
- **Tools:** Ableton + FabFilter
- **Output:** 2 sub-loops.

(Remaining short clips to be merged into an "FX Pack": doorknobs, cash register beeps, anklet sounds, etc.; Priority 3, total est. ~4–6h)

Or should I just stick to 30% and walk away if they say no? Second, the emotional side: that spike of anxiety earlier really threw off my rhythm today. 2) Drop the deposit to 15% or 20%, but strictly limit the scope to "exploratory concepts + one major revision," with the balance due upon approval. Pros: Might keep the client. Cons: Higher risk and time cost.

Trigger: Less than 4 hours of sleep for 2 consecutive nights or waking up at night with a racing heart.
Steps: 1) Immediately initiate the bedtime ritual (phone off for 1 hour, 10-minute breathing exercises); 2) Contact my therapist for an extra session or adjust next week’s schedule; 3) Postpone non-core deliverables and inform long-term clients of alternative plans.

Trigger: Hospital confirms a need for hospitalization, expensive tests, or surgery.
Steps: 1) Confirm details with parents and get a cost estimate; 2) Arrange for an escort for the visit or coordinate remotely; 3) Activate backup funds/short-term freelance plan; 4) If needed, discuss cost-sharing or help channels with family.

Single file (keep 16-bit, 44.1k)
ffmpeg -i input.aif -c:a pcm_s16le -ar 44100 -ac 2 output.wav

Batch (bash)

Subject: Deposits / Milestones — Quick Reusable Phrases & Contract Essentials (Private Memo)

**The One-Sentence Rule (Copy-Paste Ready)**
Bottom line: Collect a 30% deposit upfront; see attachment for milestones and acceptance terms.

**Red Flag Phrases (Commonly used by clients for quick screening)**
- "Budget is limited; can we try a trial collaboration first?"
- "No deposit/Let’s just try it out; pay if satisfied."
- "Let’s see the results first, then talk money."

**Copy-Paste Reply Templates (Three Tones)**
1) Firm (Default)
"Thank you for your trust. My workflow is as follows: Sign the contract and pay a 30% deposit of the total project amount. Once the deposit is received, I will schedule the timeline and kick off the work. Please see the attachment for milestones and acceptance terms. Let me know if you’d like to confirm the deposit now so I can lock in your slot?"

2) Flexible but Protected (For budget constraints)
"I understand the budget is tight. I can offer two options: A) Standard Collaboration—Sign the contract with a 30% upfront deposit; B) Small-scale Paid Trial—A ¥500 trial fee (deductible from the final contract), covering only one small layout/visual concept. Which do you prefer?"

3) Sending Contract/Payment Details (For chat)
"I’m sending over the contract and payment info. The contract outlines milestones, acceptance, and refund terms. Once confirmed, please pay the 30% deposit to the account below (or via supported payment methods). I’ll send you the first week’s schedule as soon as it clears."

**Contract Snippets (Short & Copyable)**
Payment & Acceptance: Upon signing, the Client shall pay 30% of the total project fee as a deposit (kick-off payment); 40% upon mid-term acceptance; and the remaining 30% upon final delivery and email confirmation. Acceptance is based on online/email confirmation by both parties. Late payments incur a 2% weekly penalty on the outstanding balance. If the Client cancels, the deposit is non-refundable proportional to work progress.

**Milestone Examples (Short Phrases)**
Milestone 1: Kick-off & Requirement Confirmation (Starts upon deposit receipt);
Milestone 2: Mid-term Visual Drafts & Interactive Prototypes (7-day delivery & feedback window);
Milestone 3: Final Delivery (Source files, asset handover, invoicing).

**Operational Checklist (Internal Use, Sequential)**
1. Send contract (Template link/attachment) → 2. Confirm 30% deposit payment → 3. Lock time blocks in calendar and send schedule upon receipt (include half-day weekly for personal experiments) → 4. Set up auto-allocation: Split funds into Emergency Fund/Tax/Daily Expenses (ensure home and baseline costs are covered) → 5. File naming convention: ClientAbbr_Project_Version_Status (e.g., ABC_StoreRedesign_v1_inprogress).

**Emotional Notes (Private)**
Felt a bit "tested" today during the first round of alignment when the client asked, "Budget is limited, can we try a trial?" This triggered my sensitivity regarding deposits/milestones. Reminder to self: The bottom line isn't about being difficult; it’s about protecting myself and my family’s stability (Deposit first → Schedule work). Keep the tone friendly, but never do free trials. If a compromise is needed, prioritize the "Small-scale Paid Trial" over working for free.

**Backup Phrases (For Quick Chat)**
- "I understand. If the budget is limited, we can do a paid trial (¥500, deductible later), or follow the standard process with a 30% kick-off payment. Which works better for you?"
- "To guarantee your slot and the quality of work, I require a kick-off payment upfront. I’ll send over the first week’s schedule immediately once received."

**Storage & Reuse Tips**
- Save this memo as: "Contract_Baseline_Template_2025-10-16" for easy copy-pasting.
- Pin common replies to the top of mobile notes; check before bed if any need to be sent the next day.

— Zi'ang Lin

For each delivery, please provide proof of work, including timestamped audio samples, project file lists, and screenshots of export/edit logs.

If there are no objections, please reply to confirm and complete the deposit payment within 5 business days. I will then confirm the production schedule and begin work.

These arrangements are intended to minimize process uncertainty and cash flow risks. I am open to discussing the details of these terms and look forward to finalizing this so we can move forward.

2025-03-13 20:05 — Memo / To Self

I just dropped the preliminary medical checkup and care plan into the family group chat. The reactions were realistic and direct:
- Mom asked: "Is this frequency too high? Can we afford it?" (She sounded a bit panicked, worried about the financial burden).
- Dad followed up with technical questions about insurance: "What exactly do the exclusions and waiting periods in these policies say? Will they cover these screenings or chronic disease-related costs?"

I felt a brief flash of tension—fearing the numbers would be flat-out rejected or that they’d see this as just another burden. But I reacted quickly: I suppressed my own anxiety and replied with a structured message. I focused on the logic behind the three tiers of plans (Conservative/Neutral/Aggressive) and included an actionable next-step checklist. I kept the tone as objective as possible, using bullet points and timelines to invite discussion. It felt like turning an "emotional burden" into "trackable tasks," which gave me some peace of mind.

Why I’m choosing to institutionalize care rather than express it emotionally:
- Emotionalizing makes discussions defensive or accusatory; it gets stuck in feelings and increases my parents' anxiety.
- Breaking things down into numbers, frequency, costs, and cost-sharing options makes it easier for them to accept or suggest edits item by item. Lower barrier, less resistance.
- This aligns with what I’m trying to practice: turning responsibility into a hand-offable/shareable process, rather than carrying it all on my own back.

Short-term Action Items (with specific deadlines):
1. Tomorrow (3/14) morning: Complete the draft for the three tiers of "family_health_budget_2025" (Conservative / Neutral / Aggressive). For each, list: checkup frequency, estimated total annual cost, alternative items, and priority. Name the file "family_health_budget_2025_v1," send it to my parents, and provide recommended options (Recommendation + Alternatives) in the message.
2. By 3/15: Prepare an insurance comparison list and cost-sharing proposal. List key clause comparisons for at least 3 candidate insurers (exclusions/waiting periods/claim scenarios), estimated annual costs, and attach a one-page "Proposed Cost-Sharing Plan" (e.g., I cover X%, parents cover Y%, emergency fund trigger conditions). Send this to the legal advisor to confirm the framework (see next point).
3. Within 48 hours (by the night of 3/15 at the latest): Contact the legal advisor to confirm the agreement framework: key points like deposits/milestones, information disclosure and authorization, and emergency decision-making processes. Clarify which parts need professional legal wording, then relay the suggestions back to my parents to avoid misunderstandings from verbal promises.

Personal Emotional Regulation Plan (to be executed tonight/next few days):
- Spend 15 minutes tonight (20:20–20:35) writing the three most important tasks for tomorrow at the top of my phone notes (a habit to help stop overthinking before bed).
- Do three deep breaths (4-4-6) + a cup of hot water. If I’m still restless, walk downstairs for 10 minutes and record some ambient sounds on my phone (a ritual to distract myself).
- Set phone to "Do Not Disturb" at 22:30; try not to keep staring at the group chat in bed. If I need to edit copy, do it tomorrow morning when I’m fresh (leverage morning productivity).
- Treat this emotional reaction as data: Next time I face similar family doubts, lead with three choices instead of a long explanation. Observe the effect and record changes (tag in notes as "family_convo_pattern_03_13").

Notes (To Self):
- Today’s handling was an improvement: brief anxiety → rapid structuring → proposing options. Keep managing "care" like a project; don't turn yourself into a permanent emotional buffer.
- Remember to leave an "experimental fund/flexible line item" in the plan. Don't lock in every single cost; giving my parents some breathing room will lower the probability of rejection.

2024-12-08 10:30 — Memo (To My Future Self)

**Current Feeling:** Relieved, but my mind is still racing through workflows and "what-ifs." The first sale brings instant security, but don't let that fluke go to your head—turn this fluke into a repeatable process.

**Key Records (Pragmatic, for immediate execution):**
1) **Contract Template Update (Saved)**
   - Location: 'contracts/sop_v1.docx' (Uploaded to cloud drive)
   - Key Terms: 30% upfront; 40% / 30% payments tied to delivery milestones.
   - Version Notes: v1 draft, saved 2024-12-08 10:12. Changes: Added deposits and milestone payments, payment triggers, simple breach/refund clauses, and a payment account template.
   - Next Review: 2025-01-08 (In one month) — Goal: Refine terms into copy-paste email/quote templates based on real-world feedback.

2) **Why "Upfront Payment" is Non-Negotiable (Note to self: Don't go soft)**
   - Reality Check: In the past, tight cash flow messed with judgment and delivery rhythm; scrambling for funds puts you on the defensive (magnifying stress on time, energy, and even family). Working without a deposit is too dangerous—this isn't about being rigid on principle; it’s the baseline measure to protect project kickoff and your own emotional stability.
   - Operational Meaning: For all new clients, work begins *only* after confirming the deposit has cleared (or seeing a clear online payment receipt). Reject all verbal "pay later" promises.

3) **Financial Allocation (Handling the first sale)**
   - Split the incoming funds immediately: 20% to Creative Reinvestment/Emergency Fund; set aside a percentage for tools/subscriptions and taxes (to be finalized upon actual receipt); the rest is current cash flow. Allocate before spending to reduce the anxiety of checking balances at night.
   - Filing Path: `archive/sales/2024-12-08/` (Store invoices, chat logs, transfer receipts, and contract snapshots here. Use consistent naming: `client_yyyy-mm-dd_invoice|chat|contract`)

4) **Archiving & Showcasing**
   - Archive all "Proof of First Sale" (screenshots, conversations, invoices) in the path above for future case studies or B2B marketing.
   - Portfolio: Prepare a one-page case study draft (Problem—Solution—Results screenshots). Plan to add this first sale to the "Recent Projects" section by 2024-12-15 (Keep client info anonymous/blurred; use UI samples and outcome descriptions where necessary).

5) **Next Action Items (Short-term)**
   - **Today:** Confirm `contracts/sop_v1.docx` is uploaded and backed up locally; move current payment receipts and contract copies to the `archive` path. — Check off in memo once done.
   - **Within 48 hours:** Turn "Payment Triggers" in the contract into specific items (e.g., design phase acceptance criteria, delivery formats, acceptance deadlines).
   - **Within 1 week:** Turn the contract template into a copy-paste email template and save it in drafts; ensure all quotes include the "upfront payment" clause by default.
   - **In 1 month (2025-01-08):** Review contract terms against execution issues. Adjust ratios or workflows (optimize based on evidence, don't just tinker for the sake of it).

**Emotional Reminders (For the perpetually anxious self)**
- The first sale is a win. Allow yourself to breathe, but don't mistake a moment of security for a permanent guarantee. Convert "feeling safe" into "verifiable evidence and repeatable processes."
- Stop using the bank app as a sleeping pill. Set a one-time end-of-day audit: once a day, fixed time, 15 minutes max (confirm income/expenses + necessary actions), then put the phone away.
- You have a habit of naming files `final_2` or `final_true`. This time, use clear version control and dates. Save your future self from redundant decision-making.

**Closing Thought:** Insisting on a deposit isn't about a lack of trust in the client; it’s about giving yourself the breathing room and rhythm to produce great design. By the next review, I hope to see `contracts/sop_v2` (or at least a log of v1 in action with some data to back it up).

I was on such a high tonight—finished the first batch of field recordings and was lying in bed listening to the fresh edits on loop. My mind was already racing with ideas for the cover art and promotional copy. Then I sent a few notes and a screenshot of the preview to my collaborator. He replied with a single, curt sentence. It was so cold. In that instant, all my excitement vanished. My heart started racing with intrusive thoughts: Did I mess something up? Is the budget going to be delayed again?

I didn't dare go to sleep, so I did a few things to turn that uncertainty into something controllable:
- Immediately backed up all raw files and exports to both local and cloud storage, and saved screenshots of key interfaces and chats as evidence.
- Drafted a brief project proposal outlining milestones, payment schedules, and division of labor. While writing, I listed out potential out-of-pocket expenses and the budget (I refuse to be the one passively bearing all the risk later).
- Messaged Zhao Ming to ask if he could check the wording and task distribution in the proposal. He replied quickly with two suggestions; his reassurance helped me calm down a bit.

Lying in bed now, my mind is still spinning, but I feel steadier than before. I know I’m double-checking everything because of this uncertainty—it’s an old habit of mine, a mix of anxiety and a sense of responsibility toward the project. But I need to learn to channel this energy into clear steps instead of endless spiraling.

Three things to do tomorrow (written on a sticky note, which I’ve already checked twice before bed):
1) Send the proposal to my collaborator and request a clear confirmation on milestones and payment nodes within 48 hours; screenshot the response for the record.
2) Complete the budget sheet for equipment/supplies and get quotes from two suppliers to confirm the minimum acceptable upfront cost.
3) Break the entire sampling plan into five specific dates, add them to the calendar, and send the first deadline to the team as the "signal to start."

I really want to get to sleep early, but I’ll probably scan through today’s files one more time... I'll try turning off my phone notifications first.

My bottom line: 30% deposit upfront. Payment link: https://pay.example.com/invoice/2025-10-16-01 (tested and working). Explicitly rejecting any vague payment arrangements. I’ve archived this exchange as a negotiation template: 'clients/negotiation_examples/2025-10.eml'.

**Key Terms (Ready to copy-paste into emails/contracts):**
- **Deposit:** 30% (Paid before project kickoff; scheduling begins upon receipt).
- **Milestone Payments:**
  1) **Kickoff & Wireframes** (Deliverables: Project plan + Lo-fi): 0% (Covered by deposit/used as proof of start) or as agreed (flexible).
  2) **Mid-term Delivery** (Deliverables: Mid-to-Hi-fi prototypes + interaction specs): 40% (Payable within 7 days of mid-term review approval).
  3) **Final Delivery** (Deliverables: Source files, delivery notes, and functional assets/slices): 30% (Payable within 7 days of final acceptance).
  *(Note: 30% Deposit + 40% Mid-term + 30% Final = 100%)*
- **Rush Fee:** For deliveries required within 50% of the original timeline, a 20% surcharge applies to the base quote. Deposit and milestone ratios remain the same; scheduling takes priority.
- **Changes & Cancellations:**
  - **Cancellation before kickoff:** Deposit is non-refundable (compensation for time and resource reservation).
  - **Out-of-scope changes after mid-term review:** Quoted separately based on additional workload; 50% deposit required for new work.
- **Invoicing/Tax:** Tax-inclusive/exclusive as per contract; standard VAT invoice by default (special VAT invoice subject to further discussion).
- **Acceptance:** Based on the delivery checklist and email confirmation; no response within 3 business days constitutes default acceptance (unless agreed otherwise in advance).

**Usage Instructions (When to reuse this template):**
- **Applicable Scenarios:** Single-module/short-cycle mobile UI, visual iterations, small branding assets, standalone landing pages, etc. (Best for projects ≤ 6 weeks and total value ≥ ¥3,000).
- **Non-applicable Scenarios:** Long-term monthly retainers, large-scale products requiring multi-person collaboration, or continuous delivery projects (these require monthly contracts or SOWs).
- **Quick Application Steps:** Replace [Project Name / Total Price / Milestone Dates / Payment Link / Tax Info]; confirm rush requirements before sending; save the reply as a new template (Format: clients/negotiation_examples/YYYY-MM.eml).

- During today’s monthly follow-up, I reported my recent small rituals and anti-relapse check-in practices to my therapist. Overall, my mood is steadier than last month—not the "high-highs and low-lows" kind of safety, but a cautious peace of mind that comes from knowing "as long as there’s a process, I won't completely panic."
- **Key Outcome:** Formalized several specific actions into searchable documents and schedules. It’s a form of externalized commitment (be kinder to myself; stop just looping everything inside my head).

**Executed (Fact Sheet)**
1. **Next-Day Priorities**
   - Fixed the "Most Important Thing for Tomorrow" to the very top of my phone notes (now implemented as a bedtime ritual).
2. **Nighttime Do Not Disturb (DND)**
   - Enabled DND (23:00—07:00) and added "bedtime lockout" to the workflow to reduce the impulse for mindless late-night account checking.
   - It sounds ridiculous, but I still get the urge to scroll sometimes—admitting this allowed me to use rules to pull myself back.
3. **Bank Reserve Screenshot Archiving**
   - Archived the latest bank reserve screenshot (Filename: `bank_reserve_2025-11.png`) in a local encrypted album/backup.
4. **Session Records**
   - Archived follow-up notes and process logs. Path: `health/therapy/2025_notes.pdf` (includes today’s key points and next steps discussed with the therapist).

**Experimental Plans (Defined Start/End)**
- **Two-Week "Bedtime Lockout" Experiment**
  - **Start:** 2025-11-20 (Tonight)
  - **End:** 2025-12-03 (Inclusive)
  - **Goal:** Complete three fixed tasks every night: Write next day’s MIT → Enable DND → Archive daily accounts/at least one bank screenshot (if no changes, keep the previous one).
- **Tuesday Half-Day Personal Experiment (Continuous Trial)**
  - **Phase 1 (6-week trial)**
  - **Start:** 2025-11-25 (Tuesday) 09:00
  - **End:** 2026-01-06 (Tuesday) 13:00
  - **Time Block:** Every Tuesday 09:00—13:00, reserved exclusively for personal product development/experimentation (no new gigs, no distracting minor tweaks).
  - **Goal:** Push fragmented "want-to-do but always interrupted by clients/messages" experiments into actual shippable prototypes or data.

**Self-Deprecating but Practical Notes**
- I know this sounds like I’m imposing "house rules" on myself ("back to that sticky-note obsession again"), but compared to all-night anxiety, at least a procedure turns that anxiety into measurable actions.
- If I find myself still checking accounts in the middle of the night two weeks from now, it means the rules need harder technical measures (automated sub-accounts/further backups) rather than just self-soothing mantras.

**Mood & Commitment (A Note to Self)**
- I won’t become my "ideal self" tonight, but I can turn small things into rules. If the rules are reliable, the voice of anxiety gets quieter. Staying consistent with this is more realistic than being perfect.

7) Follow-up Action List (What I need to do at the scheduled time):
   - 13:40 Send final nudge (Template: Clear deadline + deposit terms).
   - If no reply: Send one more SMS/Voice reminder after +12 hours; if still no clarity after 72 hours → Execute Plan B or C.
   - If they agree: Immediately organize payment vouchers and contract terms, place order/rent equipment, and save all screenshots to the cloud.

Mental Reminders (For myself):
- Get the math right on money and time. Don't let the urge to "fast-forward" crush your cash flow.
- This is an opportunity to validate taste and partnership stability, not a gamble. Proceed if returns are likely; stay conservative if they aren't guaranteed.
- Make the decision based on the thresholds once the time comes. Do not wait indefinitely.

**Title: Creative Experiments Quarterly Review**

**Date/Time:** 2025-06-20 (Fri) 20:00–21:30 (90 mins)
**Location:** Home / Desk
**Participants:** Just me (Private)

**Recurrence:** Every 3 months (Quarterly Review). Next: 2025-09-20 20:00
**Reminders:** 1 hour before; 1 day before at 18:00 (Mobile notification)

**Objective:** To institutionalize "small-scale, bounded creative experiments"—preserving my creative identity and experimental space without compromising rent or financial support for my parents.

**Context:** My therapist suggested incorporating creative experiments into quarterly planning. Given the need to tighten the creative fund and enforce contracts/milestones to stabilize cash flow, I will use low-cost experiments to maintain creative momentum for now.

**Agenda (90 mins)**
*   **0–10 min: Re-read Objective + Confirm Current Constraints** (Opening highlights)
*   **10–30 min: Budget Review (Current Rules)**
    *   Confirm creative fund limit: 400 RMB/month (Current hard constraint)
    *   Summarize spent/reserved amounts for this quarter (Extract from bookkeeping app)
*   **30–55 min: Results & Experiment List Review**
    *   List completed/unfinished experiments (Note cost and time invested for each)
    *   Decide which to continue and which to kill
*   **55–80 min: Next Quarter Goals & Boundary Setting (Actionable & Quantifiable)**
    *   Goal examples: 1 sound pack listed; 2 creative notes published; Maintain 1-month small experiments (Single exp. ≤ 150 RMB, ≤ 10 hours)
    *   Define experimental boundaries (See below)
*   **80–90 min: Action Breakdown + Sync to Todo List & Bookkeeping App**
    *   Identify 3 tasks for next week (Each with a deadline)
    *   Set the next review reminder

**Current Budget Constraints & Experimental Boundaries (Must be kept in event)**
*   **Monthly Creative Fund Cap:** 400 RMB/month (Priority: Rent and parents; Creativity is a secondary budget)
*   **Single Experiment Cost Cap:** 150 RMB (If exceeded, a short justification must be written and approved during the quarterly meeting)
*   **Time Investment Cap:** Max 10 hours per experiment (Log in Memos)
*   **Mandatory Output:** Must result in a clear output (publishing, packaging, or reusable assets) to avoid continuous consumption for "practice" only.
*   **Expense Tracking:** Any creative-related costs must first be tagged as "Creative/Pending Approval" in the bookkeeping app and synced to the Todo list.

**Contract / Financial Workflow (Long-term Actions)**
*   **Default for all future paid collaborations:** 30% deposit + clear milestones (suggested 2–3 stages) + late payment clauses (late fees/suspension of delivery).
*   Keep standard contract attachments in the "Templates" folder; review and update the "one-sentence version" for email intros during the next review.
*   **Debt Collection & Milestone Enforcement:** List as action items for this week (see below).

**Action List (Must complete and check off in Todo after meeting)**
*   [ ] Sync review highlights to mobile Memos (Title: Q3 Creative Experiment Review) — *Complete tonight*
*   [ ] Update bookkeeping app: Set "Creative Fund" balance to 400/month and add a new Q3 budget tag — *Within 3 days*
*   [ ] Update contract template: Add deposit and milestone sample sentences (Copy to email templates) — *Within 7 days*
*   [ ] Send milestone confirmation emails to current late-paying clients (Cite contract terms) — *Within 48 hours*
*   [ ] Send a short text to parents: Explain the temporary adjustment to the creative fund for this quarter (Be transparent, emphasize it's short-term) — *By this weekend*

**Notes (Private / Emotional Log)**
*   I’m feeling quite anxious right now (the income volatility is still weighing on me), so this "small and bounded" system fits my current state better than blind, grand attempts. Turning creativity into bounded, low-cost experiments is actually how I’ll sustain my creative identity long-term—try to believe that for now.
*   After this review, stick to the Sunday night 15-minute routine: list next week’s tasks and write the "One Key Thing" at the top of a sticky note (Keep this ritual).

**Links (For Implementation)**
*   **Memos:** /Creative Experiments/Q3 Review (Synced)
*   **Bookkeeping App:** Tag "Creative Fund" / Tag "Emergency Supplement Plan"
*   **Todo List:** Action items synced (Review Sunday night)

**Closing:** Be pragmatic. Create within limits. Don't push yourself to the edge of financial and mental exhaustion. Save the notes tonight; read the contract template one last time before bed.

November 11, 2022. Dragged my exhausted body out of bed, but the thought of having to finish that recording session from last night kept lingering. Before recording, my heart was racing, and my mind was flooded with worst-case scenarios: people moving things in the hallway, neighbors slamming doors, the room echo spiraling out of control... I was running on 30% energy, yet the pressure felt like a weight on my chest. Sure enough, once I started, I ran into the sounds of hallway moving and the sharp cracks of doors closing. I almost wanted to scrap it and start over, but I didn't give up—I moved the bookshelf half a meter to act as a baffle, stuffed a thick blanket into the door gap, and adjusted the mic position and absorption angles, noting down the parameters as I went. That feeling of turning raw emotion into a series of actionable decisions was strange yet grounding.

Listening back to the playback at the end, I whispered to myself in the room, "I'm actually quite happy with one of these takes." That sentence felt like hitting a pause button; the tension in my chest finally eased. I saved the file as *final_true_v1.wav*, labeled it "Recorded / Ready for Zhao Ming," and I'll send it to him tomorrow morning for a professional eye and some feedback. I also updated the milestone progress in my spreadsheet—pinning down these small wins makes both the cash flow and my mood feel a bit more stable.

Before bed, I stuck to the routine: wrote tomorrow’s "Most Important Thing" at the top of a sticky note and did a few minutes of breathing exercises. I don't want to force any toxic positivity right now; I just want to remember that small achievement from last night: anxiety doesn't have to be crushed; it can be managed with a pile of blankets and a few adjustments. Alright, that’s it. Keep up the little rituals, and don't let these small victories go unnoticed.

first_line_refusal_v1

—— Financial Tick (Daily ritual; fixed for Wednesday mornings; add "Today's Financial Tick" to bedtime notes) ——
1) Time/Frequency: Every Wednesday 09:00 — 11:30 (half-day buffer). Mark "Today's Financial Tick" as the first priority for tomorrow during bedtime prep.
2) Checklist (Copy & Use):
   - Accounts Receivable (Order # / Client / Amount / Due Date)
   - Projected Income vs. Fixed Expenses (Rent / Parents' allowance)
   - Clients to follow up with this week (Email date + Link/Screenshot)
   - Deposits received & Milestone status (Contract # / Amount / Completed items)
   - Bank/Payment account balances & emergency liquidity reserves
   - List of reports/invoices to be uploaded or sent
3) Action Items (Check ✔ upon completion): Follow-up emails/reminders sent / Milestones updated / Ledger screenshots archived to contract folders.

—— First-Line Refusal / Deferral Scripts (Templates for direct copy-paste) ——
Scenario Tags: Email = Formal written; Chat = WeChat/SMS; Phone = Verbal communication.

A. Email — Scope Creep / Delivery Deferral
Template (Email):
"I understand your requirements, but the current progress/timeline does not allow for this. I can provide a feasible proposal with milestones and a budget by [Date, e.g., 2024-04-26]. If you would like to prioritize this, I can adjust the original schedule, but it will require corresponding adjustments to milestones and deposits."
Context: Client requests extra features or early delivery without discussing budget or timing.

B. Chat — Lightweight Reply / Evaluation Time Needed
Template (WeChat/SMS):
"Acknowledged. I've added this to my evaluation list and can confirm by [Date, e.g., noon the day after tomorrow] at the earliest. If we need to start immediately, it will involve new milestones/extra hours; I'll send over a quote shortly."
Context: Client adds a request on the fly; need to manage expectations via chat first.

C. Phone — Quick Verbal Response
Template (Verbal):
"I see where you're coming from. I'm currently pushing through two active milestones, so I need to confirm the scope and costs of this adjustment first. I'll send over the specific dates and costs today/tomorrow, and we can schedule it once confirmed."
Context: Client pushing via phone; need to respond quickly while buying time for written confirmation.

D. Budget Constraints / Discount Requests
Template (Email/Chat):
"I understand the budget is limited. To complete this within your current budget, we would need to reduce the scope of delivery or extend the timeline. Would you like me to send a scaled-back delivery list first, or should I provide a full quote for your comparison?"
Context: Client asks for a price reduction or fee cut.

E. Immediate Refusal (High-risk / Unreliable Clients)
Template (Email):
"Thank you for the proposal, but I am unable to take on this project at this time. Should future conditions (milestone-based payments/signed contracts) be met, I would be happy to re-evaluate."
Context: Client refuses deposit, has a history of breaches, or project risk is too high.

—— Standard Follow-up Phrases (Closing lines for easy copying) ——
1) "I have added this communication to this week's Financial Tick; I will update you and send a screenshot on Wednesday." (Provides the client with a sense of timeline and verifiability)
2) "To protect both our schedules, please confirm the milestones and deposit before I proceed to the next step." (Urges written commitment)
3) "Upon receiving confirmation, I will respond with a detailed plan and delivery timeline within 48 hours." (Promises a specific response window)

Notes (Personal Reminders):
- Pin this note and name it "first_line_refusal_v1" for easy copying to Email/WeChat.
- After each use, copy the sentence into that day's contract log and save a screenshot (chain of evidence).
- If the client disagrees, use the Chat template to stabilize the situation first, then decide whether to proceed during the Wednesday Financial Tick.

- Party B shall transfer the usage rights of deliverables to the Client upon receipt of full payment. Until full payment is received, Party B retains copyrights and portfolio display rights.
7) **Scope Changes**
- For requests exceeding the original scope, both parties shall evaluate and confirm the changes, delivery timeline, and fee adjustments in writing.

- Disputes shall be settled through friendly negotiation; failing that, they shall be submitted to the jurisdiction of the courts in the place where the contract was signed.

2025-10-16T09:12:00

**Event Overview**
- Initial communication with a potential client. The client expressed budget constraints and tentatively suggested a "trial run" first, explicitly stating a refusal to pay a deposit. Simultaneously, they requested an expedited timeline while pushing for a lower quote.
- I replied with my established boundary template (requiring a deposit and milestone payments), but the client continues to push for a compromise regarding the "no deposit" issue.
- Spent the morning at my desk repeatedly checking my bank balance, feeling a brief surge of anxiety (increased heart rate, refreshing the account). I eventually pulled my emotions back into an actionable state by structuring the problem (Bottom Line / Optional Compromises / Timelines).

**My Bottom Line**
- **Deposit:** 30% (explicitly written into the contract/quote; no work begins without payment).
- **Milestones & Delivery:** Settlements on a weekly/phase basis (milestone payment ratios clearly outlined in the contract).
- **Scope of Service:** If the client requests an expedited schedule, it will be handled via an "Urgent Rate" + "Reduced Deliverable Scope."

**Feasible Options (For Negotiation)**
1) **Standard: Stick to the Bottom Line**
   - Require the 30% deposit to be settled → Start work; follow contract milestone payments.
   - *Suitability:* Prioritizes cash flow and risk control.

2) **Compromise (If I want to keep the client and they have genuine long-term potential)**
   - **Paid Trial:** A 1-day or 2-day paid exploration (Fee = 10% of the original project quote) to produce a Minimum Viable Sample. If the collaboration continues after this, the trial fee is credited toward the total, and the deposit follows original rules.
   - **Alternatively:** Reduce the initial scope (shrink to an MVP), with the deposit still set at 30% of the reduced total.

3) **Walk Away / Mark as Pending**
   - If the client insists on zero deposit and refuses a paid trial, categorize them as "Pending/Declined" and release the time block for existing long-term freelance clients or personal experiments.

**Short Template for Client (Adjusted for tone, ready to send)**
- **Gratitude + Clear Deadline**
  "Thanks for your interest. I can start within X business days once the 30% deposit is received (or I can offer a 1-2 day paid exploration package for ¥XXX, which will be credited toward the contract upon success). If you’d like to see a sample first, I can provide a paid minimum deliverable (1 day, credited to the total). Please confirm your payment intent by 10/20 18:00; otherwise, I will release this time slot to other projects. — Ziang Lin"
  *(Note: Adjust specific figures and times based on the negotiation)*

**Timeline & Owner**
- **Today (2025-10-16) 09:12** — Log this communication (Owner: Ziang Lin)
- **Today (2025-10-16)** — Decide on the "Standard/Compromise/Pending" path based on the reply; send the above template if needed (Owner: Ziang Lin)
- **2025-10-20 18:00** — Final Confirmation Deadline: Client must confirm payment method or accept the paid trial; otherwise, mark as Pending and release the time block (Owner: Ziang Lin)
- **2025-10-18** — Update contract template (add paid trial option and 30% deposit clause) and save as `negotiation_template_v1` (Owner: Ziang Lin)
- **2025-10-25** — Complete automatic bank transfer settings (automatically move deposits to emergency fund/project account) as a standard future process (Owner: Ziang Lin)

**Risks & Considerations**
- Requests for "Expedited + Discounted" work will compress the workflow and likely impact quality and delivery times. Urgent fees and deliverable scopes must be clearly defined in the contract.
- **Emotional Reminder:** This morning’s brief anxiety shows that cash flow remains a sensitive point. The deposit system and automatic transfers are key to reducing anxiety caused by these short-term fluctuations. If necessary, write this experience into a brief note for parents/friends to avoid making decisions while emotional.

**Next Steps (Internal)**
- Send the short template above (if the client continues to refuse the deposit, immediately propose the Paid Trial or mark as Pending) — **Deadline: Tonight 22:00** (Owner: Ziang Lin)
- Update the contract template and archive screenshots/key points of this conversation as `negotiation_template_v1` (Owner: Ziang Lin, **Deadline: 10/18**)
- Add the client to the CRM with a "Pending - Deposit Issue" tag for future review and reuse (Owner: Ziang Lin, **Deadline: 10/18**)

**Conclusion (Private Memo)**
- Clients who want to "try it out first" or "refuse deposits" are common. Maintain principles, but use tools like the "Paid Trial" to turn risks into testable, small steps. Standardizing today’s process protects cash flow while keeping the door open for high-potential long-term clients.

November 12, 2025, 19:30

Had a call with my parents tonight. It started with that familiar tension—Mom asking about work with worry in her voice, and I could hear Dad’s silent, suppressed stress on the other end. I walked them through my arrangements one by one: I’ve set up automatic transfers for my emergency fund (saved two copies of the screenshots), just confirmed the deposit and milestone clauses in the contract with my long-term freelance client, and scheduled the time for next week’s medical escort in my calendar (with reminders set for one day and two hours prior). At first, Dad kept asking, "What if you...", and I could feel the anxiety in his voice, that urge to pull me back onto a "stable" track.

Then, he relaxed—not a big laugh, but the heart-wrenching urgency in his voice faded. He even suggested, "Let’s go for the check-up together next week." I actually felt a lump in my throat at that moment; the weight on my chest finally lifted. I often feel like I’m carrying too much, terrified that an unexpected crisis will crush my current stability. This feeling of being seen and trusted is rare. The panic hasn't vanished entirely, but it’s clearly become manageable: there are actions, there is time, and there is company.

Recording tonight’s three "dones": 1) Auto-transfer set and screenshots archived; 2) Contract terms confirmed and client’s confirmation reply received; 3) Calendar event (medical escort) and reminders all set. Writing these down makes it feel real, not just empty talk—it’s a documented sense of security I can look back on whenever I panic again.

The plan ahead is clear: prioritize topping up the emergency fund to my target (start with a one-month auto-transfer plan, then see if I can add a lump sum from freelance rush-order income at the end of the month); list out the workflow for the check-up day (previous reports, questions to ask, what insurance covers); and maintain my usual "chain of evidence" in project communications (bank screenshots, milestone descriptions).

My anxiety dropped from a 7/10 to about a 4/10 tonight. Not because the problems are solved, but because I’ve found a rhythm that gives me peace: speak it out, document the proof, and pin down the time. Tomorrow before bed, I need to stick to the habit of writing the most important task at the very top of the sticky note—don't let new thoughts drag me away again. I can breathe a short sigh of relief today.

2023-06-26 20:15

The transfer came through. I felt a wave of relief, but it was tinged with guilt. I’ve already drafted a repayment plan and shared it in the family group chat.

When I saw the notification pop up on my phone, it felt like a small weight was lifted off my chest—not the kind of relief you celebrate, but more a sense of "don't panic for now." My voice was low when I called to say thanks; I probably sounded a bit shaky. I kept thanking them, but inside I felt ashamed: *How is it that I’m relying on someone else’s advance again?* Yet, I know the reality of my situation—self-reproach isn't going to help anyone right now.

I’ve laid out the repayment schedule and amounts clearly in the group chat; it’s a commitment to myself as much as to them. I also set a reminder in my calendar to upload the transfer receipt tomorrow morning. My therapist mentioned last week that breaking down uncertainties into concrete steps helps reduce anxiety. So, I’ve started integrating these "safeguards" into my routine: deposits, milestones, and clear invoicing processes. I have to admit, it slows things down a bit, but I feel much more grounded.

I just hope I can get some decent sleep tonight. My pre-sleep ritual: write down the single most important task for tomorrow—uploading that receipt and setting the repayment reminder to recurring. It seems like every time something like this happens, it serves as a reminder to thicken the lines of my financial boundaries. I hope this is a small turning point, rather than the start of a cycle.

3.14 A quiet announcement: I’ve finally compiled the field recordings from this week’s street sessions into the first draft of the demo. Only felt safe enough to sleep after backing up all the raw files tonight. Feeling that mix of total satisfaction and pure exhaustion 😅.

Locations include: a corner in an old bookstore (raw/bookstore_corner_01.wav), an alleyway after the rain (raw/street_rain_02.wav), and a pour-over coffee station (raw/coffee_pour_03.wav).
