October 17, 2023, 19:00

Just got off the phone with Mom. She choked up when she heard about sharing part of the costs; I could hear her swallowing hard over the line. In that moment, I felt a sharp sting in my chest and my own throat tightened for a second—it’s humiliating to realize how easily that sound drags me back into my old patterns of self-reproach. But I forced myself to pull the conversation back to the facts: the timeline, the breakdown of expenses, and viable installment plans. Then I said it: "I’m not taking everything on myself anymore; we need a distribution sheet." The atmosphere didn't improve immediately, but at least the emotion was converted into actionable next steps.

Back at my desk, I opened budget_recovery_2023.xlsx and listed out priorities and due dates. My energy feels low, but having a list in my head is slightly better than endless internal friction. I know I have a tendency to rush in and "put out fires" the moment I hear family members sounding distressed; this is the trigger chain I need to focus on deconstructing in my next therapy session.

Points for the next session (Draft):
- Immediate emotional reactions and physical sensations (racing heart, lump in throat, urge to pay immediately) when hearing parents choke up or receiving news about their illness.
- How to use scripted sentences and short protocols to reduce "on-the-spot" emotions (e.g., stating the split/timeline first before expressing emotion).
- Practical, enforceable boundaries: when to insist on deposits/milestones and when a short-term compromise is acceptable.
- Family division of labor: how to specifically split responsibilities and schedules with Dad and my sister to reduce my solo burden.

Writing these down tonight and putting the most important task at the top of my sticky notes before bed. I’m not optimistic enough to think this will be solved overnight, but at least today I wasn't completely swallowed by my emotions.

October 25, 2025  10:30 AM

When I saw my parents' physical exam reports this morning, my heart skipped a beat and my hands started shaking. I couldn't stop staring at those numbers—blood lipids, blood pressure—and my mind immediately started calculating potential medication costs, follow-up visits, further tests... Then, as if a button had been pushed, I instantly transferred money to my parents, called the hospital to book the earliest possible follow-up appointment, and told Mom I’d definitely be there this weekend to take her to the doctor.

The tension hasn't disappeared now that I've done all that, but I feel a bit steadier than I did at first; at least I’ve turned the anxiety into concrete actions. Still, I can't help wondering: how do I prepare for long-term expenses? If long-term medication or surgery is needed, will a few freelance gigs be enough? In moments like this, I blame myself for not putting money into a dedicated reserve sooner.

The next step is to see the doctor as scheduled, listen carefully to the treatment advice, and then reassess the financial burden. Meanwhile, tonight I’ll put together a simple spreadsheet for short-term cash flow and medical reserves, so I at least have an actionable plan. What I want to say to myself right now is—I’ve done what I can. I’m nervous, but I won't stop trying to solve this.

Not only would I have lacked the energy to take my parents for their follow-up appointments, but my anxiety around them would have been even worse; I might have even pushed them away out of sheer frustration with my own "incompetence." qa_items.question: A week after asking your family for financial help, your father’s transfer has already relieved the immediate crisis. So why do you feel even more guilty instead, and even haunted by a terrifying sense of losing control?
qa_items.answer: It’s a complicated feeling. While there was a definite sense of relief the moment the money hit my account, it was immediately followed by an overwhelming wave of shame—the realization that I’m still relying on my parents. I feel like at my age, I should be the one taking care of them, yet here I am, being a burden instead. Seeing my bank balance only stay afloat because of a transfer from my father makes me spiral: if my freelance work stays this unstable, how will I ever step up if their health takes a turn? This uncertainty about the future scares me far more than just being broke.

choices.text: What actually haunts me the most is that "boiling frog" sensation. While the money solved my immediate problems, I realized I’ve started habitually relying on this "family safety net." The other day, I even caught myself thinking that if things don't pick up next month, I could just find another excuse to ask for more. This fear—watching my own willpower crumble—is far more soul-crushing than being broke. I’m terrified that I’ve lost that "sink or swim" drive for good, and that I’ll end up as nothing more than a parasite, content to just live off my parents and settle for mediocrity.

This fear actually stems from a sense of frustration caused by "role displacement." At that family gathering, I had already bragged to my relatives about how well I was doing out there, even promising to buy major appliances for the house. Now, instead of succeeding, I’m forced to secretly turn to my father to bail me out. I’m terrified that one day the facade will crumble in front of my relatives. This exhaustion from maintaining my vanity, coupled with a self-esteem that feels like it could collapse at any moment, makes this money feel like a burning coal in my hands.

It’s mainly because I suddenly realized that this money was actually my parents' life savings, originally set aside to renovate their old house back home. Even though they keep saying there’s no rush, the thought of them still putting up with that leaky kitchen and dimly lit living room for my sake feels like a needle pricking my heart. The guilt of "misappropriating" their quality of life in their later years makes it impossible for me to use this money in peace. I feel like every cent I spend is overdrawing their happiness, and the weight of it is just suffocating.

It’s a complicated feeling. While there was a momentary sense of relief the second the money hit my account, it was immediately followed by an overwhelming wave of shame—the kind that comes from thinking, "I can’t believe I’m still relying on my parents." I feel like at my age, I should be the one taking care of them, yet here I am, being a burden instead. Why does it feel like you’ve cut out all your personal time lately? qa_items.answer: Sigh, how should I put this… my mindset has definitely shifted lately. I used to think that making money was all about treating myself, but now, looking at the red flags on my parents’ medical reports, I just feel this overwhelming sense of guilt. I feel like I owe them so much. Now, whenever something comes up, my first instinct is to push my own plans aside—even that "creative weekend" I’d been looking forward to for ages. Even though we’re managing to cover the medical bills for now by shuffling some funds and adjusting orders, this feeling of sacrificing my own life to fill the gaps really makes me anxious. I just can't shake the feeling that I'm still not doing enough.

Even though the financial pressure is heavier than before—and I’ve even had to dip into my emergency savings—I feel that shifting my life’s focus is worth it if it buys us peace of mind, even if it means putting my personal projects on hold for a while.

Although this "non-stop" lifestyle is exhausting, seeing them get through their checkups smoothly under my care gives me a sense of peace that no amount of books or travel could ever provide.

choices.text: Actually, this sense of resistance stems primarily from the fact that I’m wavering on my own career path. I feel like I’m stripping him of the comfort he deserves in his later years, which is why I get so incredibly frustrated whenever the topic of money comes up.

qa_items.question: Zi'ang, regarding that money your dad gave you for your cash flow back in June—if you hadn't taken it and had gritted your teeth to handle it on your own instead, how do you think your mindset would be different now?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? The thought did cross my mind at the time, but to be honest, if I hadn't taken the money, my anxiety right now would probably be way worse. Even though taking it made me feel incredibly guilty—to the point where my hands were shaking as I texted him "sorry for making you worry"—there was this undeniable sense of relief in my chest the moment I did. If I had tried to tough it out all on my own, I probably would’ve ended up taking on a bunch of soul-crushing gigs just to save up, eventually burning myself out or draining every bit of creative inspiration I have. That kind of "sense of failure" might have been even harder to swallow. Now, even though I feel like I owe my family, at least I have the headspace to plan for his follow-up appointment. Being able to actually do something for the family matters more to me right now than just being "independent" for the sake of it.

That terrifying realization—that "the child wishes to provide, but the parents are already gone"—completely rewrote my priorities. I felt that if I could trade it all for their peace and security, I would be willing to never touch a creative project again for the rest of my life. It’s a debt I owe them.

To be honest, I was mainly affected by what happened to my aunt’s family. Back then, she was too sick to afford the hospital, but when she reached out for help in the family group chat, there was nothing but silence for the longest time. In the end, it was my dad who swallowed his pride and went around begging people for money. That whole scene hit me so hard; I realized that if I don’t save up as much as I can now and spend more time looking after them, I’d never be able to forgive myself for that feeling of helpless bystander syndrome if an emergency ever struck our home. So, no matter how exhausted I am, I feel like I have to put my personal life on the back burner for now.

To be honest, that headspace was just miserable. The real tipping point was when my dad transferred me some money to help me get by, without even a second thought. I felt like such a failure—being at this age and still having to rely on my parents for financial support. Right then, I felt like even if I had to scrap all my creative projects and pull all-nighters doing low-paying freelance gigs, I had to secure my parents' health and well-being first. It felt like the only way I could make up for that sense of indebtedness.

To put it bluntly, it’s the aftermath of a deep conversation I had with my mom. She actually mentioned it jokingly at the time, saying how the neighbor’s kid had bought their parents a full commercial pension plan, and sighing that she probably wouldn't be so lucky in this lifetime. Even though she said it with a smile, my heart sank. I suddenly felt that my so-called pursuit of ideals all these years had been incredibly selfish. Since then, I’ve become obsessed with compensating them through material things and actions. I feel like if I don’t pave every single step of the way for them, I’m that "self-absorbed" ungrateful child.

choices.text: You caught me... Honestly, I’ve been feeling pretty insecure lately. With payments from two long-term clients being delayed, money has been incredibly tight. I guess it’s my way of trying to offset the guilt of not being able to provide them with a more stable life.

I guess I really am being a bit obsessive... But it’s actually because of this one time my dad went to get his prescription filled by himself. He didn't bring his medical record or previous test results, so he ended up wasting the entire day at the hospital for nothing. He was so frustrated and took it out on me when he got home. Ever since then, I’ve been paranoid. I feel like if I don’t research everything—the procedures, the routes, even the specific window number for picking up medicine—and post it all in our family group chat, I won't be able to focus on my work here if something goes wrong again. Having this sense of control is the only way I feel like I’m doing my duty as a daughter/son.

Sigh, you’re absolutely right. He suggested not rushing into medication just yet and seeing how things go with six months of lifestyle intervention first. So, these past few days, I’ve been busy ordering healthy, fat-loss meal plans for them.

The final diagnosis was acute inflammation caused by seasonal allergies. The doctor told us not to worry too much and prescribed a few IV drips. I stayed with her at the hospital for two days while she got the treatment; once the fever subsided, her levels returned to normal. It turned out to be a false alarm, but seeing all those up-arrows on the medical report at the time, I really thought it was something serious.

qa_items.question: Zi'ang, looking back on the past two years, you’ve gone from being so anxious over a minor abnormality in your parents' checkups that you’d cancel an entire creative weekend, to now managing the family’s health expenses with such precision using spreadsheets and sharing protocols. qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to think being "responsible" meant self-sacrifice—that whenever something happened at home, I had to immediately cut off my own life to step in. It ended up making me so anxious I couldn't sleep at night. Now, I’m starting to realize that taking care of my parents isn't a moral trial; it’s a "project" that needs to be managed. Institutionalizing the money transfers and putting medical checkups on the calendar—these "cold" spreadsheets actually give me a sense of security. They make me feel like things are under control, and I don't have to be constantly on edge, fearing the unexpected.

choices.text: I used to be incredibly emotional about these things; one look at the upward arrows on a medical report and I’d be on the verge of tears, feeling like my whole life had been thrown into chaos. Later, I started applying the concept of "boundaries" I learned in therapy to family matters, clarifying what falls under my responsibility and what my parents need to take on themselves. Instead of trying to micro-manage every detail, I’ve learned to let go and let them participate in their own health management. This mental "unburdening" has actually improved our relationship. I’m no longer a firefighter on call 24/7, but a calm observer.

I used to feel like this was a battle I had to fight all on my own. Every time something went wrong, I’d be running around like a headless chicken, desperately pulling strings to find specialists, and I even considered quitting my job to become a full-time caregiver. It wasn't until a support group meeting for patients' families that I saw others using insurance portfolios and third-party medical escort services to share the load. That’s when it clicked: professional matters should be left to the professionals. Now, I’ve secured commercial medical insurance for the whole family. My mindset has shifted from "fighting a lone war" to "resource integration," and that constant, looming fear of going bankrupt has finally vanished.

In the past, I lived with a "compensation complex," always trying to alleviate my guilt by buying the most expensive supplements and medical equipment. The result was often that the money was spent, yet my parents still felt I was under too much pressure. Later, after a heart-to-heart with my sister, we decided to establish a shared family decision-making mechanism where everything, big or small, is voted on in a group chat—it's no longer just me calling the shots. This shift from "material compensation" to "democratic decision-making" made me realize that communication is far more important than one-sided giving. Now, even when emergencies arise, I can face them with a sense of calm.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, I really feel this on a deep level. I used to think that taking care of my parents was some kind of heavy moral sentence; as long as I wasn't earning enough, I was "unfilial." That guilt weighed on me so much I could barely sleep. Later, I gradually realized that anxiety and self-blame don't solve anything—they just make life more of a mess. So, I tried to "institutionalize" these responsibilities. For example, I set up a shared spreadsheet to list every single expense and adjustment clearly. Now, seeing those transparent records and plans actually puts my mind at ease. It no longer feels like a bottomless pit, but a practical problem that can be solved step by step.

qa_items.answer:
To be honest, I know that feeling all too well. My advice to them would be: whatever you do, don’t shoulder all the responsibility in your head—it’s a recipe for a breakdown. The most effective solution is to adopt the "systematized" approach I hammered out. 

Start by creating a "family_health_budget" spreadsheet just like mine, listing everything clearly: the monthly allowance for your parents, check-up schedules, and insurance comparisons. Then, you absolutely must set up an automatic bank transfer. Even if the amount isn't huge, having this "visualized" workflow gives you back a sense of control. That way, when anxiety hits late at night, you can just glance at a screenshot of your records to ground yourself, instead of torturing yourself by obsessively checking your bank balance.

qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I think the core issue is that you have to "institutionalize" responsibility, rather than letting it turn into some endless moral judgment. Even though I still get anxious when my income fluctuates, seeing those transparent records and receipts makes me feel much more grounded. Don't try to shoulder everything alone; by externalizing the pressure into concrete plans and numbers, you can actually free yourself from that constant feeling of being on the verge of a breakdown.

qa_items.question: After the ups and downs of the past two years, what made you decide to turn parental care into a structured system like ‘family_health_budget_2025,’ rather than just playing it by ear as you did before?
qa_items.answer: Actually, it all started because those couple of years spent "robbing Peter to pay Paul" left me incredibly anxious. Back then, receiving transfers from my dad was a lifesaver, but the guilt and the sense of my own incompetence were suffocating. I realized that if I just went with the flow, this vague sense of responsibility would eventually turn into endless emotional burnout. So this year, I decided to treat it like a project: I set up a spreadsheet, scheduled automatic transfers, and turned all those gut-wrenching worries into concrete numbers and dates. There’s still pressure, of course, but at least I feel like things are under control now, and that gives me a lot more peace of mind.

qa_items.question: Looking back at that grueling June in 2023, when the cash flow dried up and family health scares hit all at once—how has that experience shaped the way you handle family matters now?
qa_items.answer: Man, that whole experience really shook me. By turning vague responsibilities into concrete to-do lists, I feel much more grounded, and I won't have to worry about everything falling apart the next time an emergency hits.

But lately, I’ve slowly come to realize that love and responsibility can actually be "institutionalized." By making those check-up lists and setting up automatic transfers, I’ve turned what used to be a chaotic mess of anxiety into concrete steps. This sense of control allows me to catch my breath while still leaving a little bit of room for my own creative work.

Actually, the biggest change was learning how to "ask for help." I used to feel like I had to shoulder everything at home by myself, which ended up pushing me to the brink of a breakdown. It wasn't until an old school reunion, where I heard friends in similar caregiving situations share their experiences, that I realized I didn't have to do every single thing personally. Now, I’ve hired a reliable part-time helper to take over the housework and convinced my cousin to drop by regularly to chat with my dad. This "social support" has finally allowed me to step back from the daily grind and find my own rhythm in life again.

I think my mindset has shifted from being "defensive" to being "at peace." In the past, I was always terrified of them getting sick or something happening, so I’d call several times a day just to check in, which kept everyone on edge. The turning point was a deep conversation I had with my dad last year. He told me that what he really wants is "dignity," not just "care." Since then, I’ve stopped forcing them to live by my standards. Instead, I installed a full suite of smart home monitoring equipment; as long as the data looks normal, I don’t interfere. This sense of "distance" has actually made our relationship much more relaxed than before.

qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? It’s a subtle feeling. Before, whenever I thought about my parents' health or medical bills, all I felt was this vague sense of guilt and panic. But now that I’ve broken everything down into spreadsheets and automated transfers, that "unsettled" anxiety has definitely subsided. Even though this level of institutionalization makes life feel a bit rigid—and I even have to sacrifice part of my creative fund to top up the emergency pool—at least I’m not obsessively checking my bank balance in the middle of the night anymore. For someone as emotionally sensitive as I am, that sense of security from knowing I’ve secured the baseline of my life is a real lifesaver.

qa_items.question: Zi'ang, after going through the process of arranging medical checkups and transferring money for your family, if you knew other independent designers suddenly facing the pressure of "family emergencies," what advice would you give them on balancing that sense of moral guilt with the realities of professional life?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I think the most important thing is to "institutionalize responsibility rather than emotionalize it." I used to feel like taking care of my parents was this heavy moral sentence, and the more I thought about it, the more anxious I got. But later, I tried externalizing everything into actionable plans—like setting up a Google Sheet to list out monthly transfers, check-up schedules, and insurance comparisons. Once I saw those numbers and processes physically laid out there, and even shared access with my parents, that sense of helpless guilt actually eased. I knew exactly what I was doing, and things felt under control. So, my advice would be: don't rush into self-blame. Try to translate your love for your family into a few clear spreadsheets and automated bank settings. Only when you feel grounded yourself can you avoid letting this pressure drag down your professional life.

choices.text: I think the most critical part is "building a multi-dimensional social support network." My advice would be to first extricate yourself from the pressure of this one-on-one dynamic. For instance, I reached out to several old classmates back home and even sought out local community volunteers for the elderly to "outsource" the handling of daily chores and emergency responses. When I see updates in the dedicated "Care Group" on my phone every day—even photos of volunteers taking my parents to the park—that lonely sense of guilt just vanishes. So, try to connect with the local resources you can't reach yourself. Let professionals do what they do best; only then will your professional mindset truly stabilize.

Actually, I would suggest they adopt a strategy of "staged transparent communication." I used to feel that my stress stemmed from information asymmetry, so I eventually made a special trip home to hold a formal "family financial meeting" with my parents. I laid everything out on the table: my fluctuating professional income, my future creative plans, and their expectations for retirement. Together, we reached a consensus to establish a joint savings account funded by me as a "family emergency fund," and we agreed to have in-depth discussions twice a year. This sense of partnership, built on candid communication, is more effective than any unilateral effort because it makes parents part of the solution rather than just passive recipients of help.

My suggestion is to "establish a visual emotional feedback mechanism." I realized that guilt often stems from being ignorant of the details of our parents' daily lives. To address this, I installed a smart home system and synced the real-time data from their health monitoring devices to my secondary monitor. As long as I can look up and see that their step counts, sleep patterns, and blood pressure curves are normal, I tell myself, "Everything is fine right now." This real-time data feedback provides immense psychological comfort, allowing me to refocus my energy on my design drafts. Therefore, using technology to bridge the physical distance between you and your family—turning care into a visible, timely signal—is far more effective than blind self-reproach.
